content.title: Views & Rendering
content.description:
The rendering system transforms items into DOM trees using views. This document covers everything you need to build views and understand how rendering works. For the architectural context (where rendering lives in the system), see [System Layers](item://c0c0c0c0-0091-0000-0000-000000000000).

---

## What is a View?

A view is a code item that knows how to render a particular type of item. Views have:
- `type`: [view](item://aaaaaaaa-0000-0000-0000-000000000000)
- `content.for_type`: GUID of the type this view renders
- `content.code` (JS) or `content.hob` (Hob): the rendering logic

A type can have many views (editable, readonly, compact, card). Users choose between them via context menus, and preferences are remembered.

### JS Views

Export a `render(item, api)` function that returns a DOM element:

```javascript
export async function render(item, api) {
  return api.createElement('div', { class: 'my-view' }, [
    item.content.title || 'Untitled'
  ]);
}
```

### Hob Views

Hob views are the preferred way to write new views. They use `content.hob` (compact JSON AST) and return hiccup (nested arrays describing DOM). The rendering system converts hiccup to DOM and uses morphdom for efficient updates on re-render.

```
(defn render [item api]
  [:div.my-view
    (or (get-in item [:content :title]) "Untitled")])
```

### View Specs (Declarative)

For simple views, you can use a view spec instead of writing code. A view spec is a data item with `type: view-spec` containing `ui_hints` that describe which fields to show and how. The [generic-view](item://bbbbbbbb-0000-0000-0000-000000000000) consumes these specs and assembles the UI using [field views](item://cccccccc-0000-0000-0000-000000000000) — reusable components that render individual fields (text, markdown, tags, etc.).

---

## View Resolution

When [`renderItem()`](item://f1111111-0008-0000-0000-000000000000#code?region=renderItem) is called, the system finds a view via [`resolveView()`](item://f1111111-0008-0000-0000-000000000000#code?region=resolveView):

1. **Explicit**: `viewId` parameter passed to `renderItem()`
2. **Item preference**: `item.preferredView` field
3. **Type preference**: `typeItem.preferredView` field
4. **Type chain**: Walk up `extends` chain, find first view with matching `for_type`
5. **Fallback**: [kernel:default-view](item://aaaaaaaa-1111-0000-0000-000000000000) (JSON display)

### Setting Preferences

```javascript
await api.setPreferredView(itemId, viewId);      // Item-level
await api.setTypePreferredView(typeId, viewId);   // Type-level (all items of type)
await api.setAttachmentView(childId, viewId);     // Contextual (in current container)
await api.restorePreviousView(childId);           // Undo contextual override
```

`setAttachmentView` preserves the old view config in a `previousView` field on the [attachment](item://c0c0c0c0-0005-0000-0000-000000000000) entry, allowing toggling back.

---

## Reactive Re-rendering

Views re-render automatically when the items they display change. The pipeline:

1. A view renders an item → the [render instance registry](item://f1111111-0008-0000-0000-000000000000#code?region=RenderInstanceRegistry) records: this DOM node shows item X using view Y inside parent Z
2. Something saves an item → `item:updated` event fires
3. The rendering system looks up all instances displaying that item
4. Each instance is scheduled for re-render (microtask-batched to coalesce rapid changes)
5. On flush: selector check → re-render if changed → morphdom patches the DOM

### Selectors

By default, any change to an item triggers re-renders of all views displaying it. For complex views, this is too coarse — you don't want to re-render a spatial canvas when a window's z-index changes.

**Selectors** let a view declare which slice of data it cares about. The re-render only fires when that slice changes (compared via `deepEquals`):

```
;; Hob: only re-render when the list of child IDs changes
(get-item (:id item)
  :select (fn [item] (map :id (:attachments item))))
```

Key rules:
- **Selector pins**: Once a view registers a selector for an item, later bare `get-item` calls on the same item don't widen it. The first selector is the intentional reactivity contract.
- **Bare dep fallback**: Views that don't register any selector get an implicit dependency on their own item — they re-render on any change (backward-compatible with simple views).
- **View code changes bypass selectors**: If the view item itself is edited, all instances using that view re-render regardless of selectors.

### Nested Rendering

Views can render child items via `api.renderItem()` (JS) or `(render-item id)` (Hob). Each child gets its own render instance with independent dependencies. Editing a child re-renders only that child, not the parent.

### Silent Saves

For high-frequency updates like drag position, use `api.set(item, { silent: true })` to persist without emitting events. No re-render occurs. The view updates the DOM directly during the interaction and persists the final state normally on completion. See [Silent Update Pattern](item://c0c0c0c0-0010-0000-0000-000000000000).

---

## The View API

Views receive an `api` object built in two layers: [`createAPI()`](item://33333333-1111-0000-0000-000000000000#code?region=createAPI) in kernel:core provides storage, events, and modules. Then [`enrichAPI()`](item://f1111111-0008-0000-0000-000000000000#code?region=enrichAPI) in viewport-rendering adds rendering methods. The REPL gets only the base kernel API; views get the full enriched API.

### Item Operations

```javascript
const item = await api.get(itemId);              // Read item
const items = await api.query({ type: id });     // Query items
await api.set(item);                             // Save (emits item:updated, triggers reactive re-renders)
await api.set(item, { silent: true });           // Save without events (for drag/layout)
await api.create(item, addAsChild?);             // Create new item
await api.delete(itemId);                        // Delete item
```

### DOM Creation

[`api.createElement()`](item://f1111111-0008-0000-0000-000000000000#code?region=createElement) creates DOM elements with event handler support:

```javascript
api.createElement('div', { class: 'wrapper', onclick: handler }, [
  ['h1', {}, ['Title']],       // Nested shorthand
  ['p', {}, ['Content']]
]);
```

### Rendering

```javascript
const dom = await api.renderItem(childId, viewId?, options?);  // Render a child item
await api.rerenderItem(itemId);           // Manually trigger re-render
await api.rerenderByView(viewId);         // Re-render all instances using a view
await api.rerenderByType(typeId);         // Re-render all instances of a type
```

Manual re-render calls are rarely needed — the reactive pipeline handles most cases.

### Navigation

```javascript
await api.navigate(itemId, params?);      // Navigate to item (replaces root)
await api.attach(parentId, childId);      // Add child to container (arity-detecting)
await api.detach(parentId, childId);      // Remove child from container
```

### Render Context

```javascript
const parentId = api.getParentId();       // Container this view is rendered inside
const viewId = api.getViewId();           // Current view's ID
const config = api.getViewConfig();       // Persistent config from parent's attachment entry
await api.updateViewConfig({ key: val }); // Persist view state (sort order, collapsed sections, etc.)
```

The parent ID enables context-aware behavior — inside a spatial canvas, clicking a link can open the target as a sibling window (`api.attach(parentId, targetId)`) instead of navigating away.

### View Discovery

```javascript
const views = await api.getViews(typeId);         // All views for a type
const view = await api.findView(typeId);           // Default view for a type
const view = await api.getEffectiveView(itemId);   // What view would render this item
```

---

## Cycle Detection

The rendering system tracks the render path. If an item appears twice in the ancestor chain, it's a cycle:

```javascript
await api.renderItem(itemId, null, {
  onCycle: (item) => api.createElement('div', {}, [
    'Already rendering ' + item.name
  ])
});
```

Without `onCycle`, an error is thrown with the full render path. See [Cycle-Safe Traversal](item://c0c0c0c0-0013-0000-0000-000000000000).

---

## Creating a Custom View

### 1. Create the View Item

```javascript
await api.create({
  id: crypto.randomUUID(),
  name: 'my-custom-view',
  type: api.IDS.VIEW,
  content: {
    for_type: 'target-type-uuid',
    displayName: 'My Custom View',
    description: 'Renders items as...',
    code: `
export async function render(item, api) {
  const container = api.createElement('div', { class: 'my-view' });
  container.appendChild(
    api.createElement('h1', {}, [item.content.title])
  );
  return container;
}
    `
  }
});
```

### 2. Test It

Right-click an item of the target type > View As > select your view.

### 3. Set as Default (Optional)

Use the View Settings modal, or call `api.setTypePreferredView(typeId, viewId)` from the REPL.

---

## Design Rationale

### Why Unified Views (Not Separate Renderers/Editors)?

The original system had renderers (display) and editors (modify) as separate item types. A single view concept replaced this because most views naturally want per-field mode control (some fields readonly, others editable), not all-or-nothing.

### Why Separate from Types?

Views are items, not embedded in type definitions. This means you can add a new view without modifying the type, users can create alternative views, and views are inspectable and modifiable like everything else.

### Why Morphdom for Hob Views?

Hob views produce a complete DOM tree on every render. Morphdom diffs the new tree against the existing DOM and patches only what changed, preserving scroll positions, form inputs, and focus. Old event handlers are retained (they have correct refs to the kept DOM elements).

---

## Available Views

```query
const views = await api.query({ type: api.IDS.VIEW });
views.sort((a, b) => (a.name || '').localeCompare(b.name || ''));
return views
  .filter(v => v.content?.displayName)
  .map(v => `- **${v.content.displayName}**: ${v.content.description?.split('\n')[0] || v.name}`)
  .join('\n');
```

---

## See Also

- [System Layers](item://c0c0c0c0-0091-0000-0000-000000000000) — Kernel / viewport / userland split
- [viewport-rendering](item://f1111111-0008-0000-0000-000000000000) — The rendering engine source code
- [Attachments](item://c0c0c0c0-0005-0000-0000-000000000000) — Parent-child relationships and view config storage
- [Silent Update Pattern](item://c0c0c0c0-0010-0000-0000-000000000000) — Save without triggering re-renders
- [Stale Closure Prevention](item://c0c0c0c0-0011-0000-0000-000000000000) — Fresh data in event handlers
- [Content Field Conventions](item://c0c0c0c0-0024-0000-0000-000000000000) — Standard field names (`for_type`, `ui_hints`, etc.)
